title: NASA's Cosmic Explorer Takes Flight
story: Blast off! On September 21, 1984, NASA sent a powerful new eye into the sky. The Galaxy-C satellite soared into orbit, ready to uncover secrets of the universe. This space explorer was designed to detect gamma rays, the most energetic form of light. Scientists were eager to use Galaxy-C to study explosive cosmic events like supernovas and black holes.
activity_question: What type of light was Galaxy-C designed to detect?

title: Starlight Bends: Einstein's Theory Proven
story: Cameras clicked as the sky darkened. In 1922, a team of astronomers and Nyangumarta people gathered near Broome, Australia. Their mission? Capture a total solar eclipse to test Einstein's wild idea that space itself could bend. As the moon blocked the sun on September 21, their photos revealed something amazing. Starlight curved around the sun, just as Einstein predicted. This Wallal expedition proved his Theory of Relativity, changing our understanding of the universe forever.
activity_question: What did the Wallal expedition prove?
activity_choices: The moon is made of cheese
follow_up_question: How do you think taking photos of an eclipse helps scientists learn about space?

title: Climbing to the Top at 99
story: Teiichi Igarashi was born in Japan in 1886, but his greatest adventure came much later in life. This remarkable mountaineer set an incredible record when he became the oldest person to climb Mount Fuji. At the astonishing age of 99, Igarashi conquered Japan's tallest peak on September 21, proving that it's never too late to reach new heights. His determination and spirit inspired people of all ages to pursue their dreams, no matter how challenging they might seem.

title: Swimmer Makes Waves with Historic Gold
story: A splash of victory echoed through the pool as Anthony Nesty of Suriname clinched gold in the 100m butterfly at the Seoul Olympics in 1988. His triumph on September 21 wasn't just a personal best - it marked Suriname's first-ever Olympic gold medal. Nesty's win also broke barriers as he became the first black athlete to claim individual gold in Olympic swimming. His stellar performance dashed American Matt Biondi's hopes of a seven-gold sweep.

title: Jellyfish Catch Some Zzz's
story: Scientists made a surprising discovery in 2017. They found that jellyfish, despite not having brains, can actually sleep! Researchers at Caltech observed the jellyfish Cassiopea taking naps. This groundbreaking research, published on September 21, showed that sleep might be more widespread in the animal kingdom than we thought.

title: Galileo's Final Plunge into Jupiter
story: Swoosh! On September 21, 2003, the Galileo spacecraft took its last brave dive. title: Global Team-Up for COVID Vaccines
story: Countries joined forces in 2020 to fight COVID-19! The World Health Organization announced a huge plan called COVAX. Its goal? To share vaccines fairly around the world. By September 21, nations with two-thirds of Earth's people had signed up. They aimed to give out billions of doses to those who needed them most.
activity_question: What was the name of the vaccine-distribution initiative?
follow_up_question: Why do you think it's important for countries to work together during a health crisis?

title: The Birth of a Barcode Pioneer
story: Beep! Every time you hear that sound at the store, thank Bernard Silver. Born in Pennsylvania in 1924, Silver grew up to become the co-inventor of the barcode. This clever system of lines and spaces helps track products and makes shopping faster. Silver's invention on September 21 changed how we buy things forever!
activity_question: What did Bernard Silver co-invent?
follow_up_question: How do you think stores kept track of items before barcodes?

title: A Nobel Mind Bubbles Up
story: Fizzy drinks aren't the only things that bubble! Donald A. Glaser, born in Cleveland in 1926, invented a device that used bubbles to detect tiny particles. His bubble chamber helped scientists see the invisible world of atoms, earning him the Nobel Prize in Physics. Glaser's bubbly brilliance began on September 21, sparking a lifetime of groundbreaking discoveries.
activity_question: What did Donald A. Glaser invent that used bubbles?
follow_up_question: How do you think bubbles could help scientists see tiny particles?

title: NASCAR Legend Born: Richard Childress
story: Vroom! The roar of racing engines echoed through Winston-Salem, North Carolina in 1945. On September 21, a future NASCAR superstar was born: Richard Childress. He grew up to become a Hall of Fame team owner, leading his Richard Childress Racing team to victory. Childress's cars zoomed to the top, winning multiple championships in both the Winston Cup and Xfinity Series.
activity_question: In which city was Richard Childress born?
follow_up_question: What does NASCAR stand for?

title: Astronaut Takes Flight from North Dakota
story: Reaching for the stars began in Jamestown, North Dakota for Richard J. Hieb. Born in 1955, this future space explorer would grow up to become an astronaut. Hieb blasted off on three exciting space shuttle missions. His cosmic adventures on September 21 and beyond helped expand our understanding of the universe.
activity_question: Where was Richard J. Hieb born?
follow_up_question: How many space shuttle missions did Richard J. Hieb fly on?
